title: Today's Tech Brief: Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI
summary: Exploring the impact of agentic AI on organizational design
imageAlt: Image from source article for Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI
imageCaption: Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI
imageSourceTitle: Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I'm looking at some fresh signals on the intersection of technology and organizational design. Specifically, I'm diving into the world of agentic AI and how it's changing the way we think about work and teams. It's a lot to take in, but I'm excited to share some thoughts.

One article from MIT Technology Review caught my eye. It discusses how the growing adoption of enterprise-level AI agents is creating a disconnect between ambition and execution. Apparently, 85% of organizations want to leverage agentic AI, but there's a gap in actually making it happen. I find it interesting that there's such a big disparity between wanting to use this tech and actually making it work.

What stands out to me is that agentic AI refers to AI systems that can act independently, making decisions and taking actions on their own. This is different from traditional AI, which is often more reactive. The idea is that these agentic systems can help organizations automate complex tasks and free up human workers for more strategic work. I'm curious to see how this will play out in different industries.

Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Sponsored In partnership with Ema Amid rapidly growing adoption of enterprise-level AI agents, there’s a disconnect emerging between ambition and execution. Although 85% of organizations say they want to be agentic within the next three years, 76% say their current operations and infrastructure can’t support that change. They cite a lack of readiness across... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
